segments.text: Hi, I'm Tom Howlett. I'm a product
director on the code quality domain at
Sonar.
So, we all know that AI agents are
developing code incredibly fast now and
that's working. It's functional, but is
it correct? At Sonar, that's our
specialism. We check code for
reliability, maintainability, and
security. But at the moment, you wait
for the code to be committed, going up
to the CI, waiting for a pull request,
and then you see the problems, at which
point the agent is really slowed down.
So over the last few months, we've been
developing a new product called Analysis
for Agents, which allows the agent to
analyze the code as it's writing it.
It can do this incredibly fast.
So we will return an analysis result in
one to three seconds.
Now you're probably thinking this is
like a linter and it's just doing a
shallow analysis, but no, we've
developed a service where the code is
sent up to SonarQube. SonarQube then
combines the code change that the agent
has just made with everything it knows
about the project in the first place.
All the other analysis artifacts that
it's created using dependencies and
things like that. So we can within three
seconds pull back a really deep analysis
and find real deep issues on the code.
So today I'm going to do a demo of that.
It's just available now in beta. So once
you sign up for the beta, you can use it
through the MCP service. It works with
any AI agent that supports MCP, which
they all do, but Claude Code is a fun
one to try today. So I'm going to start
off. Let's have a look at our MCP
servers.
We can see I've got a few enabled here,
including things like Jira, but we'll
jump into the SonarQube A3S1, which is
basically the SonarQube MCP, but it's
got this beta enabled on it. If we look
at the tools, we have a great list of
tools. If you haven't used the MCP so
far, they're super valuable. So we can
see our SonarQube projects. We can see
issues. We can see quality gates.
Really importantly, we can see rules.
So if we break a SonarQube rule, the
agent can get details back on what the
problem is, how it can fix it, etc. But
if we skip to the bottom to number 18,
we see this magical new one called Run
Advanced Code Analysis, and that's the
one we're going to play with today.
Before we jump into that, I'm just going
to do a little quick test to make sure
my MCP is working. So let's ask what
SonarQube projects do I have access to?
And it's slithering apparently.
And it's pulled back from the MCP
server. You can see there's a list of
projects and it's summarized that. So we
can see I've got a couple on my personal
SonarQube GitHub, and the one we're
going to use today is the Insider, and
it's already spotted that it knows we're
on the Insider project and says the
Insider project is the one we actively
use in this repo. So that's great.
Okay. Let me clear that and we'll start
with a task.
So to speed things up a bit, I've given
Claude a planning session and it's
come up with a nice plan to work on this
Insider repo. So the project Insider is
a personal project which we use to
collect insights from customers and we
can kind of get insights on them using
AI. So I'm just going to add some UI
endpoints to this now. I'm going to kick
it off now and then we'll have a look at
what it's doing. So I'm going to say
execute analytics export/import.
Okay. And it's off.
And it started. We give it a minute to
start.
And it's reading the files.
It's having a look around in typical
Claude fashion.
And let's see where it gets to there.
Okay, it's read the key files. It knows
what it's got to do, and it's telling me
I've read the codebase and I have a
clear plan. This is great. Ready for me
to start writing the schemas and tests.
So you notice there it's not just going
to jump into the code. It's going to
write tests first. So I'm going to set
that off. And while it starts doing
that, because it'll take a little while
running tests, we'll see why it's taking
that path. So if we look at the Claude
MD here, if you haven't used Claude
before, this is just its guidance on how
to work, and we're telling it here for
this project we always want to do
test-first development, TDD.
So it's writing failing tests first,
which it said it was going to do. And
you can see here as well, we've
instructed it to keep code coverage of
any change up to 80%. Now, that matches
our SonarQube quality gate, which
enforces this 80% check. So if Claude
does a good job, it's going to pass the
quality gate there.
Now, if we look down at the git workflow,
we've got a fairly standard workflow. So
never commit to main, use feature
branches. It gives some instructions on
how to create feature branches. But then
this point number three: before
completing a task that changes or writes
code, always use Analyze. Now Analyze is
a skill that I've given Claude, and
we'll have a look at that in a minute.
It says here never use the SonarQube
MCP. The skill uses the MCP, but it uses
it in quite a specific way. It's
possible for you just to install the MCP
and let Claude work with it, but it's
much better to give it instructions on
how to use it, which is what we've done
here. There's also some instructions
around PR as well, which we've built in
to make sure that it's checking the
SonarQube quality gate and reacting to
that, but we won't go into that too much
today. This is more about the Analyze
step. So let's jump back to Claude and
see how it's getting on. This looks
good. So I can see it's written a whole
bunch of tests, and normally I'd
probably have a good look at those, but
for now we'll trust they're good. And it
says yes, it's written tests, there's
five failures, so it's got failing tests
for code it hasn't written yet. And it's
saying that's the TDD red state. Are you
ready to implement? So let's implement.
And it's off. Okay. So while it's off,
let's have a look at the skills. So if
we move up into our Claude folder, we
can see Skills, and I've got this
Analyze skill here.
So let's have a quick look at that.
Skills are great to use because you can
give it specific instructions and you
can give it its own permissions. So this
skill has permissions to do everything
it needs with the tool and everything it
needs for git to be able to see the diff
that it's going to look at. And we've
got some parameters at the top here.
It's given it a hint on how to do a diff
so we get a nice consistent result
there. And then here, this Analyze step.
So it says read the file using the Read
tool and then run the analysis using
this MCP tool. Right? Run Advanced Code
Analysis. Now let's look at what it
passes in. Passes in the project key,
which it's told it is this project key
here. There's the file path that we
talked about before, and it's given an
example. The file content, which is the
content that it's testing, and then the
branch name. And when we use the branch
name, it can match that code up with a
recent analysis so that it gets really
good context and gives us really good
results.
And then after it's done an analysis for
each file, we're instructing it to try
and fix the issues. Now to fix the issue
it needs to understand the issue. So in
this case it's calling another SonarQube
MCP method which is called Show Rule and
it passes the rule that failed when it
did the analysis. Do this to understand
the rationale and recommend a fix.
There's a bit more to this as well.
I've built this so that when it does
find issues, it also records the issues
as well, and then it adds that to Claude
MD in the future to give guidelines to
stop it creating issues in the future.
So once agents start using this service
and seeing what issues they're creating,
they can then adjust their own
guidelines, or you can help them adjust
their own guidelines so they don't make
those mistakes again. And this makes it
even more efficient. So over time you
should get less and less issues coming
through and you'll go faster and faster
with AI.
Okay. So that is our Analyze skill.
Let's jump back and see how Claude is
doing now. Okay. So it has written the
implementation for the test. I won't go
into the code here. It's all looking
good. And now it's saying to run Sonar.
One thing I didn't mention here is in
the instructions we given it, we told it
to pause. Now, I wouldn't normally tell
it to pause at this stage. I'll be quite
happy, well, if you're happy to let it
run the whole thing and then present you
with code that is already clean and has
already been checked with Sonar, but for
the sake of this demo, I'm getting it to
stop so we can see the stages it's going
through. So, let's get it to run the
analysis.
Okay. So, it'll take a minute here. Now,
the analysis is actually really fast.
It's less than 3 seconds. However,
Claude does take a little while kind of
working out exactly what it's going to
send to the analysis and formatting it
and then bring it back. So it appears to
take a little bit longer. So let's see
how it's going now.
And you can see there this is Claude
sending the content of the files it's
changed to the MCP server.
That's the first one gone. It runs them
in parallel as well, so it can do all
three at once, although it seems it can
only really process them in sync.
I guess what I'm showing you here as
well is just one way to build the
skills. Now, you can obviously
experiment and come up with your own
ways. We're looking internally at Sonar
at the best ways to use these services
with different agents and we'll be
releasing kind of more guidelines around
this in the future. Obviously this is
beta at the moment. So just giving you
an example of how we think it's working
well at the moment.
Okay. So we can see — oh yes, I can see
one of the files had some issues that's
come back. It's just checking the other
files which should be there in a minute.
Okay, I can see the last file has no
issues, and it looks like we're done.
Okay, so it found one issue, which is
what I was expecting. I'm pleased. And
it's “Async functions should not contain
synchronous file operations.” Now that's
an interesting issue. That's not
something you'd get from a standard
linter. It would be quite a hard thing
to detect sometimes. But it's a sort
thing we can get from this deep Sonar
analysis that we're getting here.
So it's gone off and it called the MCP
Show Rule method. So it's understood.
It's got some good details of that issue
and how to fix it. It's got some
suggestions. So you can say the fix is
to build the CSV in memory with StringIO.
So it's come up with a strategy.
It's also doing some other checks and
it's asking me just to do, just run
another command there.
It's looking to see if it can see this
dependency for anyio, which I think is
the recommended solution to this issue.
The fun thing with working with Claude
is it will come up with a slightly
different solution almost every time. So
I never quite know what I'm going to
get. It's good fun for demos.
Okay. So the recommended library is
available.
It's adding that into its imports
and it's making some more changes.
It's made sure the dependency is pulled
in in the TOML file and now it's
reanalyzing main.py. So that's the file
that had the issue.
And let's see if it's resolved the
issue.
And there it goes. Sending it up.
It's also at the same time rerunning all
our unit tests to check our unit tests
are still working.
Okay, our unit tests are still passing.
That's great.
And now it looks like it's got the fix
and no issues came back. So it's now
going to log that issue so it doesn't
make that problem again next time.
And so it's updating our Claude — well,
it's updating the issue log first. Let's
have a look at that. It's adding it to
our issue log and now it's going to
update Claude MD,
which will basically give it some
guidance in the future. So you can see
here: never use open on a temp file.
There, that's all cool. Okay. And so now
it's given us a nice summary. It didn't
find any issues at all, which is often
the case, on three of the files. But it
did find this really critical issue on
the main file that really could have
caused some nasty bugs around
concurrency at runtime. So it's super
valuable it's found that and it's done
the fix and it's ready. It says it's
ready for the PR. Now, I'm not going to
go into the PR now. I don't think we've
really got time, but you can really see
the value here. We've managed — the agent
has done a pre-analysis.
